It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The player moved UP. Now I can see the green block is to the left of the below-center blue box. Let me check positions and plan to navigate to all three blue boxes. The goal might be to collect or activate all three blue boxes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] not in [0, 11, 12]:  # skip background and HUD
        print(f"  id={n['id']:2d} color={n['color']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Player should now be at rows 36-39, cols 29-31 (moved up from 40-43)
# The below-center blue box is at cols 32-35, rows 36-39
# So the player is now to the LEFT of the below-center blue box, same row level
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see:
- Player (green, node 9) is at rows 37-39, cols 28-31
- White block (node 7) is at row 36, cols 28-31 (above the player)
- Below-center blue box: node 8 (c frame) at rows 36-39, cols 32-35, with blue center node 10 at rows 37-38, cols 33-34
The player is now directly to the left of the below-center blue box. They're at the same row level. Let me try moving RIGHT to see if I can interact with or move through the blue box.
Actually, looking at the layout:
- Left blue box: cols 16-19, rows 28-31
- Center horizontal bar: cols 28-39, rows 28-31
- Right blue box: cols 44-47, rows 24-27
- Below-center blue box: cols 32-35, rows 36-39
- Player: cols 28-31, rows 37-39
The player is between the left blue box and the below-center blue box. Let me think about what the goal might be. The three blue-centered boxes could be targets to collect. The horizontal bar in the center might be a platform or barrier.
Let me try RIGHT to see if I can reach the below-center blue box.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at cols 28-31, rows 37-39. Blue box at cols 32-35, rows 36-39.
# Try RIGHT to approach the blue box
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
